llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: The study uses a 2×2 factorial design with AI and clinical history (CH) as factors. The AI arm provides AI predictions to radiologists. The comparison is between XO (no AI, no CH) and AI (AI assistance, no CH) to isolate the effect of AI. This is a within-subject design where radiologists diagnose patients under different information conditions.
llm_extracted.notes: Table C.6 (page 55-56) reports treatment effects on Active Time. The AI treatment coefficient is 5.937 (SE=2.347) seconds, meaning AI assistance INCREASES time by ~6 seconds relative to the control mean of 154.317 seconds. This is a within-subject design with 227 radiologists (from summary statistics table C.1). The control mean is reported as 154.317 (SE=4.990). The outcome is in seconds (level, not log-transformed). The positive coefficient indicates AI assistance slightly slows radiologists, contrary to typical productivity gains. The study's main finding is that AI assistance does not improve diagnostic quality on average (section 4.2.2), and this time result shows it also doesn't reduce time spent.
source_quotes: In total, 227 radiologists participated in our experiment.
We find that AI assistance does not improve radiologists' average diagnostic quality, even though its predictions are more accurate than 78% of them.

We also examined the effects of AI assistance and CH on time taken and a proxy for effort (clicks). The expected effects are ambiguous—more information may slow humans but AI assistance could also enable quicker assessments. The results suggests that both AI and CH slow radiologists, but only by 4% in the case of AI
